title: Mission and stakeholder expectations understood
description: The organisation's mission and the expectations of internal and external stakeholders are understood and inform cybersecurity risk management.
guidance: Document the organisation's mission statement and map it to cybersecurity priorities in the risk management strategy.
implementationGuide: Begin by documenting your organization's cybersecurity mission statement and linking it explicitly to business objectives in ServiceNow GRC under the Policy Management module. Convene a cross-functional workshop with legal, operations, and IT leadership to validate that security priorities reflect organizational risk appetite. Publish the resulting context document in Confluence and version-control it so stakeholders can see change history. Map each identified organizational objective to a corresponding control family using the NIST CSF Navigator to confirm alignment gaps are visible. Assign an owner to each objective and set a quarterly review cadence enforced through Jira recurring tickets. You are done when every control in scope can be traced back to at least one documented business objective. A common failure is treating this as a one-time exercise — without scheduled reviews, objectives drift out of sync as the business evolves.
exampleEvidence: Signed cybersecurity mission statement document cross-referenced to board-approved strategic objectives
ServiceNow GRC policy record showing policy owner, approval date, and linked business unit
Confluence page with organizational context document, change history log, and stakeholder sign-off
NIST CSF Navigator export mapping each control family to a named business objective
Jira project board showing quarterly objective-review tickets assigned to control owners
Meeting minutes from cross-functional alignment workshop with legal, operations, and IT leads

title: Internal and external context established
description: Internal and external factors that affect cybersecurity risk management are understood and managed.
guidance: Conduct a PESTLE or equivalent analysis annually and incorporate findings into the risk register.
implementationGuide: Identify all internal and external stakeholders who have a legitimate interest in your cybersecurity posture, including regulators, customers, insurers, and board members. Use ServiceNow GRC's Stakeholder Management feature to register each party with contact details, communication preferences, and escalation paths. Define stakeholder-specific communication templates in Confluence that translate technical risk language into business impact terms relevant to each audience. Conduct an annual stakeholder expectation survey and record responses to detect shifting priorities; store results in Vanta for audit traceability. Integrate stakeholder notification workflows into your incident response runbooks in PagerDuty so alerts automatically trigger the right audience. You are done when every stakeholder class has a documented expectation, a named liaison, and a tested communication path. A common failure is omitting external parties such as cyber insurers or regulators until a breach forces the conversation.
exampleEvidence: Stakeholder register in ServiceNow GRC listing internal and external parties with roles and escalation paths
Audience-specific communication templates stored in Confluence with version history
Annual stakeholder expectation survey results with documented responses and trend comparison
Vanta evidence record linking stakeholder requirements to active controls
PagerDuty incident runbook excerpt showing stakeholder notification logic and contact lists
Board presentation slide deck demonstrating cybersecurity posture in business-impact language

title: Legal and regulatory requirements understood
description: Legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements regarding cybersecurity are understood and addressed.
guidance: Maintain a regulatory obligations register reviewed quarterly; assign an owner for each applicable regulation.
implementationGuide: Catalogue all legal, regulatory, and contractual cybersecurity obligations applicable to your organization, including GDPR, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and any sector-specific mandates. Use ServiceNow GRC's Regulatory Change Management module to ingest regulatory updates from subscribed feeds and assign triage owners. Map each obligation to one or more NIST CSF controls in a compliance matrix maintained in Confluence so that coverage gaps are immediately visible. Schedule semi-annual legal reviews with your general counsel to capture new contractual requirements from enterprise agreements; track action items in Jira. Automate control testing evidence collection in Vanta to provide continuous proof of compliance against each mapped obligation. You are done when every legal and regulatory requirement has a corresponding control, an owner, and a tested evidence artefact. A common failure is relying on manual spreadsheets that become stale when regulations change.
exampleEvidence: Compliance obligation register in ServiceNow GRC with each regulation, applicable business unit, and control mapping
Regulatory change management log showing ingested updates, triage decisions, and assigned owners
NIST CSF to regulatory requirement crosswalk matrix stored in Confluence
Semi-annual legal review meeting minutes with action items tracked in Jira
Vanta automated evidence collection report showing control test results per regulatory requirement
Signed contract excerpt with security addendum and corresponding control implementation record

title: Critical objectives and dependencies identified
description: Critical objectives, capabilities, and services that stakeholders depend on are understood and communicated.
guidance: Conduct a business impact analysis (BIA) to identify critical services and their dependencies.
implementationGuide: Define the criticality tiers for your organization's products, services, and supporting processes using a documented classification scheme reviewed by business leadership. Populate a business services catalogue in ServiceNow CMDB, tagging each service with its revenue impact, regulatory sensitivity, and recovery time objective. Link each service to its dependent IT assets so that cyber risk assessments automatically surface business-impact context. Use Jira to track annual re-classification exercises, ensuring new product launches are assessed within 30 days of go-live. Align criticality tiers to your risk appetite statement so that higher-tier services receive proportionally stronger controls. You are done when every production service has a documented criticality tier with a named business owner. A common failure is launching new services without completing a criticality assessment, leaving them unprotected by appropriate controls.
exampleEvidence: Business services criticality classification policy approved by executive leadership
ServiceNow CMDB export showing each service tagged with criticality tier, RTO, and business owner
Annual re-classification review report with comparison to prior year and rationale for changes
New service onboarding checklist requiring criticality assessment completion before go-live
Jira ticket audit trail showing timely criticality reviews for services launched in the past 12 months
Risk appetite statement mapping criticality tiers to minimum required control sets

title: Resilience requirements established
description: Outcomes, capabilities, and services that the organisation depends on are understood and communicated as they relate to cybersecurity risk management.
guidance: Define RTO and RPO for critical services and document them in the business continuity plan.
implementationGuide: Identify and document all cybersecurity dependencies your organization has on external providers, including cloud platforms, SaaS applications, and managed security services. Use ServiceNow GRC's Third-Party Risk Management module to register each dependency with a risk rating and contractual commitment to availability and security. Map critical dependencies to your business continuity plan so that a provider outage triggers a documented response. Conduct annual tabletop exercises simulating loss of a critical provider dependency, facilitated with findings tracked in Jira. Maintain a real-time dependency dashboard using Grafana or equivalent to detect degradation before it becomes an outage. You are done when every critical external dependency has a risk record, a fallback procedure, and a tested recovery path. A common failure is discovering undocumented shadow-IT dependencies only after a provider incident disrupts operations.
exampleEvidence: Third-party dependency register in ServiceNow GRC with risk ratings and contractual SLA references
Business continuity plan section detailing response procedures for each critical provider dependency
Tabletop exercise after-action report simulating loss of a primary cloud or SaaS provider
Grafana or equivalent dependency health dashboard screenshot with alerting thresholds configured
Jira tickets tracking remediation actions identified during dependency risk reviews
Vendor contract excerpts confirming security and availability commitments for critical dependencies

title: Risk management objectives established
description: Risk management objectives are established and agreed by organisational stakeholders.
guidance: Obtain board or senior leadership sign-off on cybersecurity risk objectives and include in the risk management framework.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal risk management strategy document that defines your organization's risk appetite, tolerance thresholds, and the methodology for identifying, assessing, and treating cybersecurity risks. Have the document reviewed and signed by the CISO and at least one board-level sponsor, then publish it in ServiceNow GRC as the governing policy. Communicate the strategy to all business unit leaders through a structured briefing, and store acknowledgement records in Vanta. Use FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) or an equivalent quantitative framework to express risk in financial terms so executives can make informed investment decisions. Review and update the strategy annually or after any significant change to the threat landscape. You are done when risk treatment decisions across the organization consistently reference the documented strategy. A common failure is writing a strategy that sits unread on an intranet page because it was never operationalized into day-to-day risk decisions.
exampleEvidence: Signed risk management strategy document with CISO and board sponsor signatures and effective date
ServiceNow GRC policy record for the risk strategy with linked review history
Business unit leader acknowledgement records stored in Vanta
FAIR or equivalent risk quantification model output showing risk in financial terms
Annual risk strategy review meeting minutes with documented changes and rationale
Risk treatment decision log referencing the strategy for each accepted or mitigated risk

title: Risk appetite and tolerance defined
description: Risk appetite and risk tolerance statements are established, communicated, and maintained.
guidance: Document quantitative risk appetite thresholds (e.g. max acceptable loss) and review annually with the board.
implementationGuide: Translate your risk appetite and tolerance statements into quantitative thresholds — for example, maximum acceptable annualized loss expectancy or maximum number of critical findings — and publish them in ServiceNow GRC. Cascade thresholds to business units through risk appetite statements tailored to each unit's function, reviewed by their leadership. Integrate thresholds into your risk scoring model so that any risk exceeding tolerance automatically escalates to the CISO's dashboard in ServiceNow GRC. Review thresholds at least annually against changes in the threat environment, business strategy, and insurance requirements. Use Vanta to continuously monitor whether current control posture keeps risk metrics within tolerance. You are done when no risk exceeds tolerance without a documented escalation and decision. A common failure is setting tolerance thresholds that are never connected to the risk scoring model, making them decorative rather than operational.
exampleEvidence: Risk appetite and tolerance statement with quantitative thresholds approved by executive leadership
ServiceNow GRC risk scoring configuration screenshot showing tolerance-based escalation rules
Business unit risk appetite statements signed by respective unit leaders
Vanta continuous monitoring report showing current risk posture relative to tolerance thresholds
CISO risk dashboard screenshot with escalated risks highlighted above tolerance
Annual threshold review document comparing current settings to prior year with change rationale

title: Risk management approach determined
description: Organisational risk management processes are established, including how cybersecurity risk information is used in strategic and operational decisions.
guidance: Define a risk management lifecycle (identify, assess, respond, monitor) and integrate it into governance reporting.
implementationGuide: Ensure your cybersecurity risk management activities are integrated into the enterprise risk management (ERM) framework rather than operating as a siloed function. Map cybersecurity risk categories to the ERM taxonomy maintained in ServiceNow GRC so that cyber risks appear on the enterprise risk register alongside operational, financial, and strategic risks. Establish a quarterly reporting cadence where the CISO or BISO presents cybersecurity risk posture to the enterprise risk committee. Use RSA Archer or ServiceNow GRC's risk aggregation views to roll up cyber risks into the enterprise risk heat map. Document the integration in a formal charter or MOU between the cybersecurity function and the ERM team. You are done when cybersecurity risks appear on the enterprise risk register and are reviewed at the same cadence as other enterprise risks. A common failure is cyber risks being maintained only in IT-specific tools that feed no visibility to the board or ERM committee.
exampleEvidence: Enterprise risk register excerpt showing cybersecurity risks alongside operational and financial risk categories
Formal integration charter or MOU between cybersecurity and ERM functions
Quarterly risk committee meeting agenda and minutes with CISO/BISO cybersecurity briefing section
ServiceNow GRC risk aggregation view or RSA Archer heat map including cyber risk entries
ERM taxonomy document showing cybersecurity risk categories mapped to enterprise categories
Board or audit committee presentation showing cyber risk posture in enterprise context

title: Strategic direction for risk management established
description: Strategic direction that describes appropriate risk response options is established and communicated.
guidance: Produce an annual cybersecurity risk management strategy document approved by the CISO and board.
implementationGuide: Establish clear lines of cybersecurity risk ownership by assigning each risk in your register to a named individual — typically a business process owner, not an IT role — who is accountable for treatment decisions. Use ServiceNow GRC to enforce that every risk record has a primary owner and a risk treatment plan with a target date. Train risk owners on their responsibilities through role-specific modules in your LMS, and track completion in Vanta. Conduct quarterly risk owner check-ins to validate that treatment plans are on track; record outcomes in Jira. Include risk ownership performance metrics in business unit leaders' annual performance reviews to create accountability. You are done when every open risk has an active owner, a treatment plan, and evidence of recent progress. A common failure is assigning risk ownership to IT or security staff rather than the business process owners who control the resources and budget needed to treat risks.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC risk register screenshot showing named business owner assigned to each open risk

title: Lines of communication for risk established
description: Lines of communication across the organisation are established for cybersecurity risks, including risks from suppliers and other third parties.
guidance: Define escalation paths for risk reporting from operational teams to senior leadership and the board.
implementationGuide: Integrate cybersecurity risk information into strategic and operational decision-making by establishing a formal risk advisory process for major business initiatives. Require that any project above a defined cost or data-sensitivity threshold completes a cybersecurity risk assessment using the organization's standard methodology before receiving approval. Use ServiceNow GRC to gate project approval workflows on completion of the risk assessment. Publish risk-informed decision records in Confluence so that rationale is visible and auditable. Provide executive leadership with a quarterly risk trends briefing using Splunk dashboards that show emerging threats correlated to business activities. You are done when no significant business decision proceeds without documented cybersecurity risk consideration. A common failure is cybersecurity being consulted only after a project is deployed, when remediation is far more expensive.
exampleEvidence: Project intake form requiring cybersecurity risk assessment fields with cost and sensitivity thresholds defined

title: Standardised risk response methodology adopted
description: A standardised method for calculating, documenting, categorising, and prioritising cybersecurity risks is established and communicated.
guidance: Adopt a consistent risk scoring methodology (e.g. likelihood × impact matrix) and train all risk owners.
implementationGuide: Define and operationalize your organization's risk response options — accept, avoid, mitigate, transfer — with documented criteria for when each option is appropriate. Publish the risk response policy in ServiceNow GRC and ensure that every risk treatment plan explicitly states which response option has been chosen and why. Implement a formal risk acceptance process requiring CISO or above sign-off for any risk above a defined threshold; store signed acceptance records in Vanta. Integrate risk transfer activities with your cyber insurance broker by providing annual loss exposure data generated from your FAIR analysis. Review risk response decisions annually to confirm they remain appropriate given changes in the threat landscape. You are done when every risk has a documented, approved response option and no risks are implicitly accepted without sign-off. A common failure is risks defaulting to implicit acceptance because no formal response decision was ever made.
exampleEvidence: Risk response policy document defining criteria for accept, avoid, mitigate, and transfer options

title: Strategic opportunities from risk considered
description: Strategic opportunities (i.e. positive risks) are characterised and are included in organisational cybersecurity risk discussions.
guidance: Include a section for risk opportunities in the annual risk review; evaluate technology investment options against risk reduction.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal process for identifying and managing opportunities that arise from cybersecurity risk management, such as competitive advantage from strong privacy practices or premium reductions from improved security posture. Document identified opportunities in ServiceNow GRC alongside risk records to ensure they receive equal visibility. Present opportunity findings in quarterly risk committee briefings alongside risk posture metrics. Use Vanta compliance dashboards to generate evidence of security maturity that can be shared with customers and prospects as a differentiator. Partner with the business development team to translate cybersecurity certifications — such as SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 — into sales collateral. You are done when at least one cybersecurity opportunity is formally tracked per quarter and linked to a business outcome. A common failure is treating risk management as purely defensive, missing the value that strong security posture can generate.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC opportunity register with entries linked to cybersecurity posture improvements
Quarterly risk committee briefing slide showing cybersecurity opportunities alongside risk metrics
Vanta compliance dashboard shared with prospects as part of vendor security review responses
SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificate leveraged in customer-facing sales materials
Business development team email or case study referencing security posture as deal differentiator
Annual report section attributing revenue or retention benefit to cybersecurity investment

title: Leadership roles and responsibilities defined
description: Organisational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk and fosters a culture that is risk-aware, ethical, and continually improving.
guidance: Ensure the CISO reports to the board or C-suite and has clear authority over the cybersecurity risk programme.
implementationGuide: Define and document cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for leadership, the security team, IT operations, and all business units using a RACI matrix that covers every major security process. Publish the RACI in ServiceNow GRC's Policy Management module and require annual acknowledgement from all named role holders. Align the RACI to your organizational chart and update it within 30 days of any structural change; use Jira change management tickets to enforce this. Conduct role clarity workshops for new managers and during major reorganizations using materials stored in Confluence. Include cybersecurity responsibility language in all relevant job descriptions reviewed by HR. You are done when every critical security process has a named accountable party and all role holders have acknowledged their responsibilities. A common failure is RACI matrices that list team names rather than individual owners, creating diffusion of accountability.
exampleEvidence: Cybersecurity RACI matrix covering all major security processes with named individual owners

title: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities assigned
description: Roles, responsibilities, and authorities related to cybersecurity risk management are established, communicated, understood, and enforced.
guidance: Publish a RACI chart for all cybersecurity roles and update it with every significant organisational change.
implementationGuide: Ensure that senior leadership is visibly accountable for cybersecurity by having the board or equivalent governing body approve the cybersecurity strategy, risk appetite, and budget annually. Document board-level cybersecurity oversight responsibilities in the corporate governance charter, and publish board meeting minutes that reference cybersecurity as a standing agenda item. Use Vanta to generate a quarterly board cybersecurity briefing package that translates technical metrics into financial risk and business impact language. Assign a board-level cybersecurity committee or designated director with subject-matter accountability, documented in committee charters. Provide evidence that the board reviews and approves material cybersecurity investments and major risk acceptance decisions. You are done when board meeting minutes consistently reflect active cybersecurity governance, not just annual check-ins. A common failure is delegating all cybersecurity oversight to the IT committee, removing it from the board's line of sight.
exampleEvidence: Board-approved cybersecurity strategy and risk appetite statement with board resolution on file
Corporate governance charter excerpt naming cybersecurity as a standing board responsibility
Board meeting minutes from the past four quarters showing cybersecurity as a recurring agenda item
Quarterly board cybersecurity briefing package generated from Vanta with business-impact metrics
Board cybersecurity committee charter with named director accountable for oversight
Board approval record for material cybersecurity budget allocation or major risk acceptance decision

title: Adequate resources allocated
description: Adequate resources are allocated commensurate with the cybersecurity risk strategy, roles, responsibilities, and policies.
guidance: Include cybersecurity resource requirements in the annual budgeting cycle and track against planned expenditure.
implementationGuide: Allocate dedicated cybersecurity budget and ensure adequate staffing by benchmarking your organization against industry peers using data from sources such as Gartner or SANS. Document the budget allocation process in a written policy requiring CISO input into the annual planning cycle, stored in ServiceNow GRC. Track headcount against approved positions in your HRIS and flag vacancies exceeding 60 days in Jira for escalation. Implement a skills gap analysis annually using frameworks such as NICE or SFIA to identify capability shortfalls, and fund training through platforms such as SANS or Coursera. Report resource adequacy metrics — open positions, training completion rates, tool coverage ratios — to leadership quarterly. You are done when cybersecurity headcount and budget are documented, benchmarked, and reviewed on a defined cadence. A common failure is treating cybersecurity as an IT overhead cost rather than a dedicated risk management investment, leading to chronic under-resourcing.
exampleEvidence: Approved cybersecurity budget allocation document with year-over-year comparison and CISO sign-off
Industry benchmarking report (Gartner or SANS) comparing organization's spend to peer group
HRIS report showing cybersecurity headcount versus approved positions with vacancy age tracking
Annual skills gap analysis using NICE or SFIA framework with funded training plan attached
Jira vacancy escalation tickets for positions open beyond 60-day threshold
Quarterly resource adequacy report presented to leadership with headcount, training, and tool metrics

title: Cybersecurity integrated into HR practices
description: Cybersecurity is included in human resources practices.
guidance: Embed security responsibilities in job descriptions and performance objectives for all relevant roles.
implementationGuide: Establish clear cybersecurity policies that define expected behaviours for all personnel and enforce accountability through disciplinary processes. Publish policies in ServiceNow GRC with versioning and require annual acknowledgement from all employees tracked in your LMS. Align policy language to relevant regulatory requirements using the compliance matrix maintained in Vanta. Conduct periodic policy exception reviews — at least quarterly — and require CISO approval for any active exception, with a defined expiry date. Integrate policy compliance into the HR performance management process so that violations carry documented consequences. You are done when every employee has acknowledged current policies, all exceptions are formally approved and time-limited, and policy violations result in documented disciplinary action. A common failure is maintaining policies that are never updated, creating gaps between documented expectations and actual practice.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC policy library with version history, effective dates, and employee acknowledgement completion rates

Policy exception register with CISO approval signatures, risk rationale, and expiry dates for all active exceptions

Policy review cycle schedule with documented updates made in the past 12 months

title: Policy established and communicated
description: Cybersecurity policy is established based on the organisational mission, stakeholder expectations, and risk management strategy and is communicated and enforced.
guidance: Publish a top-level cybersecurity policy on the intranet and require annual acknowledgement from all staff.
implementationGuide: Develop and maintain a comprehensive cybersecurity policy suite that covers all major domains — access control, incident response, asset management, data protection, and third-party risk — with each policy linked to its governing regulatory requirement. Use ServiceNow GRC's Policy Management module as the system of record, enforcing that every policy has an owner, a review date, and a current approval status. Conduct structured policy reviews annually or within 30 days of a significant regulatory, technology, or threat-landscape change; document each review in Jira. Communicate policy updates to affected personnel through targeted notifications and require acknowledgement within 14 days, tracked in your LMS. Perform quarterly gap analyses comparing your policy library to NIST CSF controls using Vanta to surface missing or outdated policies. You are done when every NIST CSF control area is covered by at least one current, approved, and acknowledged policy. A common failure is allowing policies to expire or go un-reviewed because no one owns the review schedule.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC policy library showing all policies with owners, review dates, and approval status
Policy-to-NIST CSF control crosswalk matrix with coverage gap analysis
Jira policy review tickets with completion records for the past 12 months
LMS acknowledgement completion report showing employee policy acceptance within the required timeframe
Vanta quarterly gap analysis report comparing policy coverage to NIST CSF control requirements
Policy update notification records sent to affected personnel with acknowledgement timestamps

title: Policy reviewed and updated
description: Cybersecurity policy is reviewed, updated, communicated, and enforced to reflect changes in requirements, threats, technology, and organisational mission.
guidance: Review all security policies at least annually or following major incidents, regulatory changes, or significant business changes.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal policy exception management process that documents every deviation from cybersecurity policy, including the business justification, compensating controls, risk acceptance, and expiry date. Build the exception workflow in ServiceNow GRC so that requests automatically route to the appropriate approver based on risk severity. Require CISO or deputy sign-off for any exception that increases risk above the documented tolerance threshold. Review all active exceptions quarterly and automatically expire those that have passed their end date; send reminders to owners 30 days before expiry using PagerDuty or ServiceNow notifications. Report the volume and risk profile of active exceptions to the risk committee monthly. You are done when the exception register is complete, current, and reviewed on a defined cadence with no exceptions surviving past their expiry date. A common failure is granting exceptions verbally or informally, which makes them invisible to auditors and allows them to persist indefinitely.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC exception register with all active exceptions showing justification, compensating controls, approver, and expiry date

CISO sign-off records for exceptions above the risk tolerance threshold
Quarterly exception review report with expired exceptions closed and renewed exceptions re-approved
PagerDuty or ServiceNow notification log showing expiry reminders sent 30 days before exception end dates
Monthly risk committee exception summary report showing volume and risk profile trends

title: Supply chain risk management programme established
description: A cybersecurity supply chain risk management programme, strategy, objectives, policies, and processes are established, agreed to by organisational stakeholders, and integrated into the broader supply chain risk management programme.
guidance: Develop a third-party risk management policy and assign an owner responsible for the supplier risk programme.
implementationGuide: Develop a supply chain cybersecurity policy that establishes minimum security requirements for all third-party vendors, suppliers, and partners who access your systems or process your data. Register all third parties in ServiceNow GRC's Third-Party Risk Management module with a risk tier assigned based on data sensitivity and access level. Require every vendor above tier 2 to complete an annual security questionnaire aligned to SIG Lite or equivalent, and store responses in ServiceNow GRC. Conduct on-site or virtual assessments for tier-1 vendors annually using CrowdStrike Falcon or equivalent telemetry to validate claimed controls. Embed minimum security requirements in all new contracts reviewed by legal, and track contract compliance in Jira. You are done when every active vendor is registered, risk-tiered, and has a current assessment on file. A common failure is onboarding new vendors without completing a security assessment, typically because procurement and security work in separate processes.
exampleEvidence: Supply chain cybersecurity policy document approved by executive leadership with third-party scope defined
ServiceNow GRC third-party register showing all vendors with assigned risk tiers and assessment status

Jira tracker showing contract compliance review actions for active vendor agreements

title: Supplier cybersecurity requirements defined
description: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for suppliers, customers, partners, and other third parties are established, communicated, and coordinated internally and externally.
guidance: Define minimum cybersecurity requirements for suppliers by tier and include them in contract schedules.
implementationGuide: Establish a vendor selection and onboarding process that incorporates cybersecurity due diligence before any new supplier relationship is formalized. Integrate a security review gate into the procurement workflow in ServiceNow GRC so that purchase orders above a defined threshold cannot be approved without a completed security assessment. Use BitSight or SecurityScorecard to conduct automated external security ratings checks on prospective vendors before onboarding. Maintain a preferred vendor list in ServiceNow GRC of pre-approved suppliers who have passed full assessment, streamlining procurement for low-risk categories. Train procurement staff on security due diligence requirements annually, with completion tracked in your LMS. You are done when no new vendor above the risk threshold is onboarded without a completed and documented security review. A common failure is business units engaging vendors directly and bypassing procurement controls, leaving security unaware of new relationships.

title: Supply chain cybersecurity practices integrated
description: Cybersecurity is integrated into supply chain risk management processes.
guidance: Perform security risk assessments before onboarding new suppliers and at contract renewal for Tier-1 suppliers.
implementationGuide: Define and enforce cybersecurity requirements in all third-party contracts, including data handling standards, incident notification timelines, right-to-audit clauses, and minimum control requirements aligned to your supply chain policy. Maintain a contract management repository in ServiceNow GRC that tracks security clause status for every active vendor agreement. Have legal counsel review all contracts above a defined value or data-sensitivity threshold for completeness of security language before execution. Implement a contract renewal workflow in ServiceNow GRC that automatically flags security clause reviews 90 days before expiry. Conduct annual contract compliance spot-checks for tier-1 vendors and document findings in Jira. You are done when every active contract with a material vendor contains documented cybersecurity requirements and has been reviewed for compliance in the past 12 months. A common failure is legacy contracts that predate security requirements and are renewed without updating security language.

title: Suppliers assessed
description: Suppliers are known and prioritised by criticality.
guidance: Maintain a tiered supplier register (Tier 1/2/3) based on data access and operational dependency.
implementationGuide: Maintain comprehensive situational awareness of your supply chain by continuously monitoring the security posture of critical third-party suppliers using automated external risk scoring tools. Configure BitSight or SecurityScorecard to send alerts when a critical vendor's score drops below your defined threshold, and route alerts to the vendor risk team via PagerDuty. Conduct quarterly supply chain risk reviews using the ServiceNow GRC third-party register, aggregating assessment results, contract compliance status, and external rating trends. Participate in sector-specific threat sharing groups such as FS-ISAC or H-ISAC to receive early warning of supply chain attacks targeting your vendor ecosystem. Document supply chain risk findings in your enterprise risk register with treatment plans and escalation decisions. You are done when critical vendor security posture is monitored continuously and any degradation triggers a documented response within your defined SLA. A common failure is relying solely on annual assessments, missing security incidents that occur between review cycles.

title: Requirements included in agreements
description: Requirements to address cybersecurity risks in supply chains are established, prioritised, and integrated into contracts and other types of agreements with suppliers and other relevant third parties.
guidance: Include a standard cybersecurity schedule in all supplier contracts covering data handling, breach notification, and audit rights.
implementationGuide: Identify and classify all components and dependencies in your software and hardware supply chain, including open-source libraries, commercial software, firmware, and hardware sourced from third parties. Implement a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) process requiring SBOMs for all internally developed and procured software, stored in a repository such as Dependency-Track. Integrate SBOM analysis into your CI/CD pipeline using tools such as Snyk or Syft to automatically detect vulnerable or malicious components before deployment. Map hardware supply chain dependencies in ServiceNow CMDB, tagging each asset with its country of origin and supplier risk tier. Subscribe to CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) feed and cross-reference it against your SBOM inventory weekly. You are done when every production software component has a current SBOM and every new deployment triggers an automated supply chain component check. A common failure is treating SBOM as a documentation exercise without connecting it to operational vulnerability management workflows.
exampleEvidence: SBOM repository in Dependency-Track showing all production software components with version and provenance data
CI/CD pipeline Snyk or Syft scan log showing component analysis results for a recent deployment

CISA KEV cross-reference report showing weekly comparison against SBOM inventory
SBOM policy document requiring SBOMs for all internally developed and procured software

title: Hardware assets inventoried
description: Inventories of hardware managed by the organisation are maintained.
guidance: Use a CMDB or asset management tool to maintain an up-to-date hardware inventory, reconciled quarterly.
implementationGuide: Maintain a comprehensive and continuously updated inventory of all physical devices — servers, workstations, laptops, network equipment, IoT devices, and mobile devices — connected to or associated with your environment. Deploy a network discovery tool such as Tenable.io or CrowdStrike Falcon to perform continuous passive and active discovery, feeding results into ServiceNow CMDB as the authoritative asset register. Configure CMDB sync jobs to run at least daily and alert on newly discovered devices that are not in the approved inventory within 4 hours. Enforce a device registration process requiring assets to be logged in ServiceNow CMDB before connecting to the network, gated by your NAC solution. Conduct quarterly physical asset audits comparing CMDB records to physical observation, resolving discrepancies in Jira. You are done when every connected device has a corresponding CMDB record with a named owner and classification, and new devices are detected and registered within 4 hours. A common failure is CMDB records becoming stale because automated discovery runs independently of the CMDB without reconciliation logic.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow CMDB asset inventory report showing all physical devices with owner, classification, and last-seen date
Tenable.io or CrowdStrike Falcon discovery scan report with newly detected device alerts
CMDB sync job log showing daily reconciliation runs and discrepancy counts
NAC configuration screenshot showing device registration gate before network access is granted
Quarterly physical asset audit report with CMDB comparison and Jira tickets for discrepancies
Alert log showing new device detection and registration completion within the 4-hour SLA

title: Software assets inventoried
description: Inventories of software, services, and systems managed by the organisation are maintained.
guidance: Implement software asset management (SAM) tooling that auto-discovers installed applications on all managed endpoints.
implementationGuide: Maintain a complete and authoritative inventory of all software applications, including installed software, SaaS subscriptions, open-source components, and licensed commercial software, across your environment. Use CrowdStrike Falcon's software inventory or Microsoft Endpoint Manager to collect installed software data from all managed endpoints, feeding results into ServiceNow CMDB daily. Implement a SaaS management platform such as Torii or BetterCloud to discover and catalogue all SaaS applications accessed by employees, including shadow IT. Conduct monthly reconciliation of discovered software against the approved software list maintained in ServiceNow GRC, flagging unauthorized applications in Jira for remediation. Integrate software inventory data with your vulnerability management tool (Tenable.io) to automatically surface unpatched applications. You are done when every production software application is catalogued with an owner, a version, and a patch status, and no unauthorized software survives more than 30 days after discovery. A common failure is failing to capture SaaS and browser-based applications, which represent a growing attack surface invisible to endpoint agents.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow CMDB software inventory report listing all applications with version, owner, and patch status
CrowdStrike Falcon or Microsoft Endpoint Manager software discovery export for managed endpoints

Monthly unauthorized software remediation report with Jira ticket closure evidence

title: Network and data flows mapped
description: Representations of the organisation's authorised network communication and internal and external data flows are maintained.
guidance: Produce and maintain network architecture diagrams and data flow maps, updated at least annually.
implementationGuide: Identify, document, and maintain a current map of all network communication flows, including internal east-west traffic, external ingress/egress, and inter-system data flows, to support risk assessment and segmentation decisions. Use Cisco Secure Network Analytics, Darktrace, or equivalent NDR tools to baseline normal traffic patterns and generate flow maps. Maintain network architecture diagrams in Confluence or Lucidchart, updated within 30 days of any infrastructure change through a change management process enforced in Jira. Overlay your network flow map with your data classification scheme to identify where sensitive data traverses the network, flagging unexpected paths for investigation. Conduct semi-annual network architecture reviews with your infrastructure and security teams to validate flow maps against actual traffic data. You are done when all production network flows are documented, mapped to data classifications, and reviewed on a defined cadence. A common failure is network diagrams that reflect the intended design rather than actual traffic, discovered only during an incident investigation.
exampleEvidence: Network architecture diagram in Confluence or Lucidchart updated within the past 30 days of last infrastructure change
Cisco Secure Network Analytics or Darktrace traffic baseline report showing normal east-west and ingress/egress flows
Data flow map overlaying network flows with data classification labels for sensitive data paths
Jira change management tickets triggering diagram updates for recent infrastructure changes
Semi-annual network architecture review report with team sign-off and findings documented

title: External systems catalogued
description: Inventories of services provided by suppliers are maintained.
guidance: Maintain a cloud services and external system register with owner, data classification, and criticality rating.
implementationGuide: Identify and inventory all external services, cloud accounts, and internet-facing systems used by your organization, including managed cloud environments, SaaS applications, and partner-connected systems. Use a Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool such as Wiz, Prisma Cloud, or Microsoft Defender for Cloud to continuously discover cloud resources across AWS, Azure, and GCP accounts. Maintain an external attack surface inventory in Tenable.io Attack Surface Management or equivalent, updated continuously, with alerts for newly exposed assets. Register all cloud accounts and external service subscriptions in ServiceNow CMDB with named owners and data classification tags. Conduct monthly external surface reviews to identify shadow IT or unapproved external services, tracking remediation in Jira. You are done when every external-facing system and cloud service is inventoried, owned, and classified, with continuous monitoring for new exposures. A common failure is cloud accounts created by developers outside the standard provisioning process, creating blind spots in the inventory.
exampleEvidence: Wiz, Prisma Cloud, or Microsoft Defender for Cloud CSPM inventory report showing all discovered cloud resources

title: Assets prioritised
description: Assets are prioritised based on classification, criticality, resources, and impact on the mission.
guidance: Assign criticality tiers (Critical/High/Medium/Low) to all assets based on BIA outcomes.
implementationGuide: Classify all assets — hardware, software, data, and services — by their criticality and sensitivity to enable risk-proportionate protection. Develop and publish an asset classification policy using a tiered scheme (e.g., critical, high, medium, low) with clear criteria for each tier, stored in ServiceNow GRC. Require asset owners to classify their assets in ServiceNow CMDB during onboarding and review classifications annually. Integrate classification data into your risk management, vulnerability management, and incident response processes so that critical assets receive prioritized treatment. Use Tenable.io to correlate asset criticality with vulnerability severity to produce a risk-prioritized remediation queue. You are done when every asset in CMDB has a current classification tag and vulnerability remediation SLAs are enforced based on asset criticality. A common failure is allowing asset owners to self-classify without oversight, resulting in systematic under-classification that distorts risk prioritization.
exampleEvidence: Asset classification policy in ServiceNow GRC with tiered criteria and owner responsibilities defined
ServiceNow CMDB report showing all assets with current classification tags and last review dates

title: Data inventoried
description: Inventories of data and corresponding metadata for designated data are maintained.
guidance: Maintain a data inventory mapping data types to storage locations, owners, and classification labels.
implementationGuide: Identify and inventory all data assets — structured, unstructured, and in transit — across your environment, including data stores, databases, file shares, cloud storage buckets, and data transferred to third parties. Deploy a data discovery and classification tool such as Microsoft Purview, Varonis, or BigID to continuously scan storage systems and classify sensitive data by type (PII, PHI, financial, confidential). Maintain a data inventory in ServiceNow GRC linked to the owning system's CMDB record, with data classification, retention schedule, and processing purpose documented. Review the data inventory quarterly and update it within 30 days of launching a new data processing activity; track reviews in Jira. Align your data inventory to privacy regulations — GDPR Article 30, CCPA — using the mapping maintained in Vanta. You are done when every production data store is catalogued with classification, owner, and retention schedule, and new data processing activities are registered before going live. A common failure is data inventory existing only on paper while actual data stores multiply in cloud object storage without registration.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Purview, Varonis, or BigID data discovery scan report showing classified data assets by type and location

title: Vulnerabilities identified
description: Vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded.
guidance: Run authenticated vulnerability scans monthly across all in-scope systems and track findings to closure.
implementationGuide: Establish a continuous vulnerability identification process that covers all assets in your inventory, ensuring that every endpoint, server, network device, cloud workload, and application is scanned on a defined cadence. Deploy Tenable.io or Qualys as your primary vulnerability management platform, configured to perform authenticated scans of all networked assets at least weekly. Integrate scan results into ServiceNow GRC's Vulnerability Response module to automate risk scoring and assignment to asset owners. Ensure cloud workloads are covered by integrating your CSPM (Wiz or Prisma Cloud) findings into the same vulnerability management workflow. Define and enforce SLA timelines for remediation by severity — critical: 15 days, high: 30 days — tracked in Jira with escalation automation. You are done when scan coverage reaches 100% of known assets weekly, and SLA compliance is measured and reported monthly. A common failure is having strong perimeter scanning but no authenticated scanning of internal workloads, leaving the highest-value targets invisible.
exampleEvidence: Tenable.io or Qualys authenticated scan configuration showing scan schedules and asset coverage percentage

Monthly vulnerability management metrics report presented to leadership with coverage and SLA trends
Authenticated scan credential configuration evidence showing internal workload scanning is enabled

title: Threat intelligence received
description: Cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources.
guidance: Subscribe to at least two threat intelligence feeds and ensure the information is actioned by the SOC within agreed SLAs.
implementationGuide: Establish formal processes for ingesting, analysing, and acting on cyber threat intelligence from multiple sources to proactively identify threats relevant to your environment. Subscribe to sector-specific ISACs (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC), CISA advisories, and commercial threat intelligence feeds from providers such as Recorded Future or Mandiant. Integrate threat intelligence feeds into your Splunk SIEM using STIX/TAXII connectors to automate indicator ingestion and alert enrichment. Assign a threat intelligence analyst or team to review and triage incoming intelligence daily, documenting findings and relevance assessments in Confluence. Correlate threat intelligence with your asset inventory and MITRE ATT&CK framework to identify techniques being used against similar organizations that your controls may not detect. You are done when every material threat intelligence advisory is assessed for relevance within 24 hours and relevant findings trigger a documented response action. A common failure is subscribing to intelligence feeds but never operationalizing them, treating them as a feed to read rather than a source of actionable decisions.
exampleEvidence: ISAC membership certificate and recent threat advisory received and actioned within the past 30 days
Splunk SIEM threat intelligence connector configuration showing active STIX/TAXII feeds

title: Threats identified
description: Internal and external threats to the organisation are identified and recorded.
guidance: Maintain a threat register populated from threat intelligence, penetration testing, and incident history.
implementationGuide: Identify and analyse internal and external threats to your organization by maintaining a current threat model that covers adversary types, attack vectors, and target assets. Use the MITRE ATT&CK framework to structure your threat model, mapping known adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) to your assets and existing controls. Review and update the threat model at least annually and after significant changes to the business or threat landscape, with updates tracked in Jira. Supplement the threat model with red team or penetration testing findings from firms such as Mandiant or CrowdStrike Services, conducted annually. Share threat model outputs with the security operations team so that detection logic in Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel is tuned to prioritized TTPs. You are done when a current threat model exists, is reviewed on a defined cadence, and detection rules in your SIEM are mapped to the threats identified. A common failure is producing a threat model as a compliance artefact that is never consulted when tuning detection rules or prioritizing controls.
exampleEvidence: Threat model document using MITRE ATT&CK framework mapping TTPs to assets and existing controls

title: Potential impacts evaluated
description: Potential impacts and likelihoods of threats exploiting vulnerabilities are identified and recorded.
guidance: Conduct a formal risk assessment for all Critical and High assets at least annually using a documented methodology.
implementationGuide: Assess the potential impacts of identified threats and vulnerabilities on your organization by completing risk assessments that quantify likelihood and consequence in business terms. Use a standardized risk assessment methodology — such as FAIR or NIST SP 800-30 — documented in your risk management policy and applied consistently. Conduct risk assessments for all new significant projects and systems before deployment, and for all tier-1 and tier-2 assets annually; track assessments in ServiceNow GRC. Quantify financial impact where possible using FAIR analysis to enable comparison with risk treatment costs and inform insurance coverage decisions. Aggregate risk assessment findings into a risk register maintained in ServiceNow GRC, with each risk assigned an owner and a treatment plan. You are done when every material risk has a current quantified assessment, an owner, and a treatment plan, and new systems are assessed before going live. A common failure is conducting qualitative-only assessments that cannot be compared to financial thresholds, making it impossible for executives to prioritize investment rationally.
exampleEvidence: Risk assessment methodology document (FAIR or NIST SP 800-30) approved by CISO and published in ServiceNow GRC

title: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts prioritised
description: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to understand inherent risk and inform risk response prioritisation.
guidance: Use a risk scoring matrix and rank risks by inherent score to prioritise remediation resources.
implementationGuide: Prioritize risks and vulnerabilities for treatment based on a combination of likelihood, impact, asset criticality, and threat intelligence context, rather than severity score alone. Configure Tenable.io's Predictive Prioritization or Kenna Security's risk score to incorporate exploitation probability and asset criticality into your remediation queue. Maintain a documented prioritization methodology in ServiceNow GRC that defines how scores translate to remediation SLAs and escalation thresholds. Review the top 20 risks in the enterprise risk register monthly with the risk committee to validate prioritization decisions. Use threat intelligence from Recorded Future or MITRE ATT&CK to adjust prioritization when active exploitation of a technique is observed in the wild. You are done when remediation resources are demonstrably focused on the highest-impact risks, not simply the most recently discovered. A common failure is prioritizing by CVSS score alone, which ignores exploitability, asset context, and business impact, misallocating remediation resources.
exampleEvidence: Tenable.io Predictive Prioritization or Kenna Security risk score configuration showing prioritization factors

title: Risk responses chosen and prioritised
description: Risk responses are chosen, prioritised, planned, tracked, and communicated.
guidance: Assign a risk response (accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid) to every rated risk and track to closure in the risk register.
implementationGuide: Communicate risk assessment findings to decision-makers in a timely, actionable format that enables informed risk treatment decisions. Publish a risk register in ServiceNow GRC accessible to asset owners, business unit leaders, and executive management, with role-based views tailored to each audience. Generate automated monthly risk reports using ServiceNow GRC or Splunk dashboards, distributed to the risk committee and board cybersecurity committee. Establish a risk escalation process defining triggers — such as discovery of a critical vulnerability with active exploitation — that require immediate notification to the CISO and escalation path documented in your incident response runbook. Train risk owners on how to interpret risk reports and make treatment decisions using materials stored in Confluence. You are done when risk findings are consistently reviewed at the appropriate level within defined SLAs and treatment decisions are documented. A common failure is risk reports being generated but not reviewed, because they are too technical for business audiences or lack clear calls to action.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow GRC risk register with role-based views for asset owners, business leaders, and executives
Automated monthly risk report distribution record showing recipients and delivery timestamps

title: Improvements identified from evaluation
description: Improvements are identified from evaluations.
guidance: Run quarterly ISMS review meetings; feed audit findings and incident lessons learned directly into the improvement backlog.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal improvement management process that captures, tracks, and drives closure of identified improvements to your cybersecurity program, drawing inputs from risk assessments, audits, incident post-mortems, and vulnerability findings. Use Jira as the system of record for all improvement actions, with each item requiring a named owner, priority classification, target date, and traceability to its source finding. Conduct a monthly improvement review meeting with the security leadership team to assess progress, re-prioritize items, and escalate blockers; document meeting outcomes in Confluence. Integrate improvement actions identified in ServiceNow GRC risk assessments and audit findings automatically into Jira through a defined workflow. Report improvement program metrics — open items, overdue rate, average time to close — to the risk committee quarterly. You are done when every identified improvement is tracked with an owner and due date, overdue actions are actively escalated, and the program shows measurable reduction in open findings over time. A common failure is capturing improvement actions in isolated spreadsheets or email threads that have no governance, leading to chronic unresolved findings.
exampleEvidence: Jira improvement action backlog showing all open items with owners, priorities, and target dates

title: Improvements from security tests
description: Improvements are identified from security tests and exercises, including those done in coordination with suppliers and relevant third parties.
guidance: Conduct annual penetration tests and tabletop exercises; capture findings in a tracked remediation plan.
implementationGuide: Ensure that lessons learned from cybersecurity incidents are systematically captured, communicated, and translated into program improvements. Require a post-incident review (PIR) for every incident above a defined severity threshold, completed within 14 days of closure, using a standard template stored in Confluence. Facilitate PIRs with representatives from security operations, IT, legal, and affected business units; record findings and improvement actions in Jira. Track all PIR-generated improvement actions through to closure in Jira, with monthly progress reviews by the security leadership team. Share sanitized PIR summaries with relevant stakeholders to build organizational learning; distribute through your security newsletter or all-hands updates. Integrate PIR findings into updates of runbooks, detection rules in Splunk, and training materials in your LMS. You are done when every qualifying incident has a completed PIR, all improvement actions are tracked to closure, and evidence shows that runbooks and detections have been updated based on findings. A common failure is completing PIRs but never acting on the improvement actions, allowing the same incident patterns to recur.
exampleEvidence: Post-incident review template in Confluence with required fields for findings, root cause, and improvement actions

title: Improvements from execution of cybersecurity plans
description: Improvements are identified from execution of operational and risk management plans.
guidance: Review and update the cybersecurity improvement plan at least quarterly based on plan execution metrics.
implementationGuide: Establish a continuous improvement process for your cybersecurity program that uses metrics, benchmarks, and external assessments to identify gaps and drive enhancement over time. Conduct an annual NIST CSF maturity assessment using a tool such as CISA's CSF assessment tool or a qualified third-party assessor, scoring each subcategory against your target maturity profile. Track maturity scores and target profiles in ServiceNow GRC, and use gaps to populate the Jira improvement backlog. Benchmark your program against peers using resources such as the CIS Benchmarks, Gartner peer insights, or sector-specific maturity models. Present annual maturity trend data to the board cybersecurity committee alongside investment requirements to achieve target maturity. You are done when a current maturity assessment exists, gaps are tracked as improvement actions, and year-over-year maturity trends are reported to executive leadership. A common failure is conducting maturity assessments without linking findings to funded improvement initiatives, producing a scorecard with no corresponding action.
exampleEvidence: Annual NIST CSF maturity assessment report with scores per subcategory and comparison to target maturity profile
ServiceNow GRC maturity tracking record showing current and target scores with gap analysis

title: Identities managed
description: Identities and credentials for authorised users, services, and hardware are managed by the organisation.
guidance: Implement an identity management system with automated provisioning/de-provisioning tied to HR systems.
implementationGuide: Implement a rigorous identity lifecycle management process covering provisioning, modification, and deprovisioning for all user and service accounts across your environment. Use Okta or Microsoft Entra ID as your identity provider, enforcing automated provisioning via SCIM connectors to downstream applications so that access follows role assignments rather than manual requests. Require that all new account requests are submitted through ServiceNow's access request workflow, approved by the account owner's manager, and fulfilled within a defined SLA. Configure automated deprovisioning workflows that trigger within 24 hours of a termination event received from your HRIS (Workday or SAP), disabling and then archiving accounts. Conduct quarterly access reviews in Okta Access Certifications or Vanta to validate that active accounts are still required and appropriately scoped. You are done when every account has a traceable provisioning record, deprovisioning occurs within 24 hours of termination, and quarterly access certifications are completed with documented outcomes. A common failure is orphaned service accounts accumulating because automated deprovisioning is only configured for human user accounts.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Microsoft Entra ID SCIM provisioning configuration screenshot showing connected downstream applications

title: Users, services, and hardware authenticated
description: Identities are proofed and bound to credentials based on the context of interactions.
guidance: Enforce MFA for all user accounts accessing systems holding sensitive data.
implementationGuide: Enforce the principle of least privilege across all user, administrator, and service accounts by granting only the minimum permissions required to perform defined job functions. Conduct a role-based access control (RBAC) design exercise using Okta or Microsoft Entra ID to define permission sets aligned to job roles, and audit direct permission assignments for elimination. Use CyberArk or HashiCorp Vault to manage privileged account access, enforcing just-in-time (JIT) elevation for administrative tasks with full session recording. Integrate your identity governance tool (SailPoint or Saviynt) with ServiceNow CMDB to ensure that access entitlements are linked to asset ownership and business justification. Run quarterly entitlement reviews using Vanta to identify and revoke excessive permissions within a defined SLA. You are done when no account holds more permissions than its documented role requires, JIT elevation is enforced for all privileged access, and quarterly entitlement reviews are completed. A common failure is allowing standing privileged access to persist because implementing JIT elevation is operationally disruptive — this is a risk that must not be deferred.
exampleEvidence: RBAC design documentation mapping permission sets to job roles in Okta or Microsoft Entra ID
CyberArk or HashiCorp Vault JIT elevation configuration with session recording enabled

title: Users, services, and hardware authorised
description: Access to physical and logical assets is authorised, incorporating the principles of least privilege and separation of duties.
guidance: Apply RBAC with least-privilege defaults and review permissions quarterly for all critical systems.
implementationGuide: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts, with phishing-resistant MFA enforced for privileged and remote access scenarios. Configure Okta or Microsoft Entra ID to require MFA for all application sign-ins, using phishing-resistant methods such as FIDO2 hardware keys (YubiKey) or certificate-based authentication for administrator accounts. Define conditional access policies in Okta or Entra ID that block access from non-compliant devices or untrusted networks, even with valid credentials. Monitor MFA bypass attempts and authentication anomalies in your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) and alert the SOC within 15 minutes of detection. Conduct semi-annual MFA coverage audits using Vanta to identify accounts without MFA enrolled and enforce enrolment within 14 days. You are done when MFA coverage is 100% for all accounts, phishing-resistant MFA is enforced for all privileged access, and bypass attempts are detected and alerted. A common failure is permitting legacy authentication protocols such as SMTP AUTH or IMAP that bypass MFA entirely, creating a persistent backdoor.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Microsoft Entra ID MFA policy configuration showing enforcement for all applications and conditional access rules
Phishing-resistant MFA enrolment report (FIDO2 or certificate-based) for all administrator accounts

title: Identity assertions protected
description: Identity assertions are protected, conveyed, and verified.
guidance: Use signed, short-lived tokens (e.g. OAuth 2.0 / OIDC) for service-to-service authentication and validate them on every request.
implementationGuide: Protect identity assertions and authentication credentials by enforcing strong credential policies, encrypting credential stores, and hardening authentication infrastructure against attack. Configure your Okta or Microsoft Entra ID password policy to enforce minimum length of 16 characters, complexity requirements, and breach password detection (using HaveIBeenPwned integration or equivalent). Enforce TLS 1.2 or higher for all authentication flows and disable weak cipher suites on all identity providers and federation endpoints. Use CyberArk or HashiCorp Vault to store and rotate service account credentials and API keys automatically, eliminating hardcoded credentials in code. Monitor for credential stuffing and password spraying attacks in Splunk using authentication anomaly detection rules tuned to your environment's baseline. You are done when all credential stores are encrypted, no hardcoded credentials exist in code repositories, and authentication anomalies are detected and alerted within defined SLAs. A common failure is rotating human account passwords while leaving service account and API key credentials static for years.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Microsoft Entra ID password policy configuration showing minimum length, complexity, and breach detection settings
TLS configuration audit report confirming TLS 1.2+ enforcement and weak cipher suite disablement on identity endpoints

title: Access permissions managed
description: Access permissions, entitlements, and authorisations are managed, incorporating the principles of least privilege and separation of duties.
guidance: Implement a formal access request and approval process; certify entitlements quarterly via access reviews.
implementationGuide: Control access to physical and logical assets based on verified identity, need-to-know, and role authorization, and log all access attempts for audit and investigation purposes. Implement network access control (NAC) using Cisco ISE or Aruba ClearPass to enforce device compliance and identity verification before granting network access. Segment your network by function and sensitivity, enforcing access between segments through firewall policy managed in Palo Alto Networks Panorama or equivalent. Configure access logging for all critical systems and forward logs to Splunk for centralised retention and search, ensuring logs are retained for at least 12 months. Conduct semi-annual logical access reviews using Vanta or SailPoint to validate that access permissions match current role requirements. You are done when all access to sensitive systems is authenticated, authorized, and logged, with log retention meeting your regulatory requirements. A common failure is deploying access controls on the perimeter but not enforcing them between internal network segments, allowing lateral movement after initial compromise.
exampleEvidence: Cisco ISE or Aruba ClearPass NAC configuration showing device compliance and identity enforcement rules
Palo Alto Networks Panorama firewall policy showing inter-segment access rules based on role and need-to-know
Splunk log ingestion configuration confirming access logs from all critical systems with 12-month retention

title: Physical access managed
description: Physical access to assets is managed, monitoring physical access is performed.
guidance: Deploy badge access logs for all secure areas and review for anomalies monthly.
implementationGuide: Verify the integrity of hardware and software components before deployment and during operation to prevent tampering and supply chain attacks. Enforce Secure Boot on all servers and endpoints, configured through your endpoint management platform (Microsoft Endpoint Manager or Jamf) and validated by CrowdStrike Falcon's device health telemetry. Implement application allowlisting using CrowdStrike Falcon or Carbon Black to prevent execution of unauthorized software, validated against a known-good baseline. Use code signing for all internally developed software, with signing certificates managed in HashiCorp Vault and enforcement verified in your CI/CD pipeline. Monitor for integrity violations in Splunk by ingesting CrowdStrike Falcon telemetry and alerting on unsigned binary execution or Secure Boot failures. You are done when Secure Boot is enabled on all managed devices, application allowlisting covers all servers, and unsigned binary execution alerts are responded to within defined SLAs. A common failure is enforcing integrity controls on servers but excluding developer workstations, which then become the vector for introducing malicious code into the build pipeline.

title: Awareness activities conducted
description: Personnel are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform general tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind.
guidance: Deliver annual security awareness training with phishing simulations; track completion rates by department.
implementationGuide: Deliver role-appropriate cybersecurity awareness training to all personnel at onboarding and at least annually thereafter, with content updated to reflect current threats. Deploy your awareness training programme through an LMS such as KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness Training, or Workday Learning, ensuring all employees complete the mandatory curriculum within 30 days of hire and annually. Supplement annual training with monthly phishing simulations using KnowBe4 or Proofpoint, measuring click-through rates and reporting trends to leadership quarterly. Tailor training content to specific roles — developers receive secure coding modules, finance staff receive business email compromise awareness — with completion tracked separately. Integrate awareness metrics into your Vanta compliance dashboard to provide continuous evidence of training programme health. You are done when all employees have completed mandatory annual training, phishing simulation click-through rates show a downward trend, and role-specific modules are assigned and tracked. A common failure is delivering generic training that does not address the specific threats most likely to target each audience, reducing its effectiveness.

title: Individuals with privileged roles are trained
description: Individuals with privileged roles are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform relevant tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind.
guidance: Require role-specific training for all privileged users (admins, developers) covering secure configuration and incident handling.
implementationGuide: Ensure that personnel with elevated cybersecurity responsibilities — including security operations, incident responders, developers, and privileged users — receive advanced role-specific training commensurate with their responsibilities. Define a training curriculum for each high-risk role, mapping required courses to skills frameworks such as NICE or SANS, and publish the curriculum in your LMS. Require security operations staff to complete annual training from providers such as SANS, Offensive Security, or vendor-specific courses (CrowdStrike University, Splunk Education), with completion tracked in Vanta. Conduct annual tabletop exercises for incident response teams using scenarios from MITRE ATT&CK, facilitated by an external provider such as Mandiant or Dragos where relevant. Verify that developer security training covers OWASP Top 10, secure design principles, and your internal secure SDLC policy, with completion required before accessing production code repositories. You are done when every high-risk role has a documented curriculum, completion is tracked, and annual exercises validate practical competency. A common failure is allowing specialist training to lapse for technical staff because operational demands take priority, eroding the skill base that defends the organization.

title: Data-at-rest protected
description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-at-rest are protected.
guidance: Enable full-disk encryption on all endpoints and database-level encryption for all stores holding Confidential+ data.
implementationGuide: Protect data at rest across all storage systems — on-premises databases, file servers, cloud object storage, and endpoint devices — using encryption appropriate to the data classification. Enforce full-disk encryption on all endpoints using BitLocker or FileVault, managed through Microsoft Endpoint Manager or Jamf, with compliance status reported to CrowdStrike Falcon for centralized visibility. Encrypt all cloud storage buckets and database instances using platform-native encryption (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Cloud KMS), with customer-managed keys for data classified as confidential or above. Deploy Microsoft Purview or Varonis to continuously monitor for sensitive data stored in unencrypted or improperly protected locations, with findings alerting the data security team via ServiceNow GRC. Maintain an encryption key management policy requiring key rotation at least annually and break-glass procedures for key recovery. You are done when all data classified as confidential or above is encrypted at rest, encryption is continuously monitored, and key management procedures are documented and tested. A common failure is encrypting primary data stores while leaving backups and log archives unencrypted, exposing sensitive data through secondary channels.
