title: Data-in-transit protected
description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-transit are protected.
guidance: Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all internal and external communications; prohibit unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP).
implementationGuide: Protect data in transit across all network paths — internal, external, and between cloud services — using current encryption standards that prevent interception and tampering. Enforce TLS 1.2 or higher for all application traffic, API calls, and inter-service communications, validated through Qualys SSL Labs or equivalent scanning integrated into your CI/CD pipeline. Use a web application firewall (WAF) such as Cloudflare or AWS WAF to enforce HTTPS on all external-facing endpoints and detect protocol downgrade attacks. Configure Palo Alto Networks or equivalent firewall to block clear-text protocols (FTP, Telnet, HTTP) from traversing production network segments. Monitor for clear-text data transmission using your NDR tool (Darktrace or Cisco Secure Network Analytics) and alert the SOC on detection. You are done when all production data in transit is encrypted with TLS 1.2+, clear-text protocols are blocked on production segments, and monitoring confirms no clear-text sensitive data transmission. A common failure is enforcing HTTPS at the perimeter while allowing clear-text communication between internal microservices, exposing data to lateral movement attacks.

title: Data-in-use protected
description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-use are protected.
guidance: Apply DLP controls and memory protection to prevent unauthorised access to data being actively processed.
implementationGuide: Protect data in use by implementing controls that prevent unauthorized access to data while it is being processed, and monitor for anomalous data access patterns. Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) controls in Microsoft Purview or Forcepoint DLP to detect and block unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data from endpoints and cloud services. Implement privileged access management controls for database access using CyberArk, requiring JIT elevation for DBAs and logging all queries against sensitive tables in Splunk. Use User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA) in Splunk UEBA or Microsoft Sentinel to detect anomalous data access patterns — such as a user downloading 10x their normal data volume — and alert the SOC. Enforce data masking and tokenization for sensitive fields in non-production environments using tools such as Delphix or IBM Optim. You are done when DLP policies cover all sensitive data egress paths, UEBA detects anomalous access, and non-production environments contain no live sensitive data. A common failure is protecting data at rest and in transit but leaving data in use unprotected, making it accessible to malicious insiders or compromised privileged accounts.

title: Backups maintained
description: Backups of data are created, protected, maintained, and tested.
guidance: Implement 3-2-1 backup strategy with encrypted off-site copies; test restores quarterly against defined RTO.
implementationGuide: Ensure that backup and recovery processes protect data integrity and availability, enabling timely restoration of critical systems and data following an incident. Define recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for all critical systems in your business continuity plan, stored in ServiceNow GRC and reviewed annually. Implement automated backups using Veeam, AWS Backup, or Azure Backup for all critical systems, configured to meet defined RPO targets, with backup jobs monitored in your SIEM. Enforce the 3-2-1 backup rule — three copies, two different media types, one offsite or cloud — and encrypt all backups using keys managed in HashiCorp Vault or cloud KMS. Test backup restoration at least quarterly for critical systems, documenting test results in Jira with completion sign-off by the system owner. You are done when all critical systems have automated backups meeting RPO targets, backups are encrypted, and quarterly restoration tests demonstrate RTO compliance. A common failure is running backup jobs without testing restoration, discovering during an incident that backups are corrupted or incomplete.

title: Networks and environments protected
description: Networks and environments are protected from unauthorised logical access and usage.
guidance: Segment networks, enforce deny-by-default firewall rules, and deploy IDS/IPS on key network segments.
implementationGuide: Implement network segmentation and protective technologies that restrict the spread of incidents and limit the impact of a compromise. Design your network architecture using a zero-trust model, enforcing micro-segmentation between workloads using Palo Alto Networks Prisma Cloud or VMware NSX to limit lateral movement. Deploy CrowdStrike Falcon's network containment capability so that compromised endpoints can be isolated within minutes of detection without requiring physical access. Configure Palo Alto Networks Next-Generation Firewall with strict application-layer allow-lists between segments, blocking all traffic not explicitly required, validated against your network flow baseline. Test containment capabilities quarterly through purple team exercises using MITRE ATT&CK lateral movement techniques, documenting results in Jira. You are done when micro-segmentation is enforced between all critical workload tiers, endpoint containment can be executed within 5 minutes of a confirmed compromise, and quarterly tests validate effectiveness. A common failure is flat network architectures that allow an attacker to move freely between systems once the perimeter is breached.

title: Technology architecture manages risk
description: The organisation's technology assets are protected from environmental threats.
guidance: Conduct a resilience architecture review annually; address single points of failure for critical services.
implementationGuide: Implement controls that ensure the environment can be restored to a known-good state following an incident, minimizing the window of exposure and enabling confident recovery. Maintain golden image libraries for all standard system configurations using Packer or AWS EC2 Image Builder, version-controlled in a dedicated image registry and rebuilt monthly. Enforce infrastructure-as-code (IaC) for all cloud resources using Terraform or AWS CloudFormation, stored in a version-controlled repository (GitHub Enterprise) so that any environment can be reconstructed from code. Implement configuration drift detection using AWS Config, Azure Policy, or CIS-CAT Pro to identify and alert on deviations from the known-good baseline within 24 hours. Test environment reconstruction from IaC at least annually as part of disaster recovery exercises, documenting results in Confluence. You are done when all production environments can be reconstructed from version-controlled IaC, drift detection alerts within 24 hours, and annual recovery tests demonstrate successful restoration. A common failure is relying on manual runbooks for environment reconstruction, which are inevitably out of date when needed most.

title: Mechanisms to achieve resilience implemented
description: Mechanisms are implemented to achieve resilience requirements in normal and adverse situations.
guidance: Define and implement redundancy (HA/DR) for all Critical and High systems per their RTO/RPO requirements.
implementationGuide: Implement capabilities to isolate compromised systems and remove threats from your environment in a controlled manner that preserves forensic evidence. Deploy CrowdStrike Falcon with network containment and process blocking capabilities, enabling analysts to contain a host in under 5 minutes directly from the console. Establish documented isolation playbooks in Confluence for each major platform type (Windows endpoint, Linux server, cloud workload, network device), reviewed and tested quarterly. Use Cortex XSOAR or Splunk SOAR to automate initial containment actions — such as disabling accounts, isolating endpoints, and revoking certificates — triggered by high-confidence detections. Preserve forensic evidence during containment by configuring memory dump and log preservation steps in your automated playbooks before any remediation actions alter system state. You are done when containment can be executed within defined SLAs, playbooks are current and tested, and forensic evidence is preserved automatically as part of the containment workflow. A common failure is reimaging systems before collecting forensic evidence, eliminating the opportunity to understand root cause and persist the threat actor's access.

title: Adequate resource capacity ensured
description: Adequate resource capacity is ensured to support cybersecurity.
guidance: Set capacity thresholds at 80% for all critical infrastructure and review usage trends monthly.
implementationGuide: Maintain adequate capacity and resilience in your infrastructure to absorb disruptions — including denial-of-service attacks, hardware failures, and incident-related load spikes — without losing availability of critical services. Deploy DDoS protection using Cloudflare Magic Transit or AWS Shield Advanced for all internet-facing services, configured with traffic scrubbing thresholds aligned to your availability SLAs. Implement auto-scaling for critical cloud workloads using AWS Auto Scaling or Azure VMSS, tested annually to confirm capacity expands under load within defined RTO. Design for high availability with redundant components, load balancing, and multi-availability-zone deployments for all critical services, documented in architecture runbooks in Confluence. Conduct capacity planning reviews quarterly using Splunk or Grafana dashboards to identify resources trending toward saturation before they impact availability. You are done when DDoS protection is active for all internet-facing services, auto-scaling is tested and functional, and capacity reviews show no critical resource trending toward saturation. A common failure is deploying redundant architecture but failing to test failover, discovering during an incident that the redundant path does not work as designed.

title: Configuration management performed
description: Configuration management practices are established and applied.
guidance: Define and enforce hardened baselines (CIS Benchmarks) for all OS and application platforms via IaC or configuration management tooling.
implementationGuide: Establish and maintain configuration baselines for all technology platforms — endpoints, servers, network devices, and cloud resources — hardened to an industry standard such as CIS Benchmarks or DISA STIGs. Publish approved baseline configurations in Confluence and manage them as code in a version-controlled repository (GitHub Enterprise), requiring change management approval for any modification. Deploy CIS-CAT Pro or Microsoft Azure Policy to continuously assess all systems against their applicable baseline, forwarding results to ServiceNow GRC Vulnerability Response for tracking. Automate configuration remediation for common drift items using Ansible or AWS Systems Manager State Manager, reducing manual remediation overhead. Review and update baselines annually and when a CIS Benchmark major version is released, tracked in Jira. You are done when all systems show greater than 90% compliance with their applicable baseline, drift is detected within 24 hours, and automated remediation handles the majority of common deviations. A common failure is publishing baseline standards without deploying continuous compliance assessment, making compliance status invisible until the next manual audit.

title: Software maintained
description: Software is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk.
guidance: Patch Critical CVEs within 48 hours, High within 7 days; automate OS patching where possible.
implementationGuide: Manage the full software lifecycle — from approved procurement through deployment, patching, and decommission — to ensure only authorized, patched software runs in your environment. Implement an application allowlist using CrowdStrike Falcon or Carbon Black that permits only software on the approved software list maintained in ServiceNow CMDB. Manage patch deployment for operating systems and applications using Microsoft Endpoint Manager (MECM), Jamf, or AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager, enforcing SLAs of 15 days for critical patches and 30 days for high-severity patches. Integrate Tenable.io vulnerability scan results with your patch management workflow so that newly discovered vulnerable software automatically generates a patching task in Jira. Conduct monthly patch compliance reports to the security leadership team, escalating any critical patches approaching SLA breach. You are done when patch SLA compliance exceeds 95% for critical and high findings, unauthorized software is blocked, and patch status is visible in real time. A common failure is patching endpoints thoroughly but neglecting server operating systems and third-party applications, which remain the most exploited attack surface.

title: Hardware maintained
description: Hardware is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk.
guidance: Follow manufacturer lifecycle guidance; replace EoL hardware before vendor support ends.
implementationGuide: Manage hardware throughout its lifecycle — from procurement and asset registration through maintenance and secure disposal — to prevent unauthorized hardware from entering your environment and sensitive data from leaving on retired assets. Require all hardware procurement to route through an approved vendor list and register assets in ServiceNow CMDB within 48 hours of receipt, enforced by your asset management policy. Implement port control on all endpoints using CrowdStrike Falcon or Microsoft Endpoint Manager to disable unauthorized USB and peripheral connections, with exceptions requiring CISO approval. Conduct annual physical hardware audits comparing CMDB records to physical observation, resolving discrepancies within 14 days. Enforce secure data destruction for all retired hardware using NIST 800-88 methods, with a certificate of destruction retained in ServiceNow GRC for audit purposes. You are done when every active hardware asset is registered in CMDB, unauthorized ports are blocked, and retired assets have certificates of destruction on file. A common failure is disposing of end-of-life hardware through ad hoc channels without data destruction, exposing sensitive data from recovered drives.

title: Log records generated
description: Log records are generated and made available for continuous monitoring.
guidance: Centralise all logs in a SIEM with minimum 12-month retention and validate log completeness quarterly.
implementationGuide: Implement comprehensive logging across all critical systems so that security events, user actions, and system changes are recorded, retained, and available for investigation and compliance purposes. Define a logging policy specifying which events must be logged for each system category, aligned to your regulatory requirements (PCI DSS log requirements, NIST SP 800-92), published in ServiceNow GRC. Forward all security-relevant logs to Splunk SIEM using Universal Forwarders or the Splunk HTTP Event Collector, configuring index retention to meet your 12-month minimum requirement. Enable audit logging on all cloud control planes (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor Activity Logs, GCP Cloud Audit Logs) and ingest into Splunk. Monitor log pipeline health daily — including forwarder status, event rate baselines, and index gaps — alerting the SOC if any source stops sending for more than 15 minutes. You are done when all defined log sources are ingested into Splunk, retention meets regulatory requirements, and pipeline monitoring detects source failures within 15 minutes. A common failure is enabling logging on all systems but storing logs locally where they can be tampered with or lost during an incident.

title: Installation and execution of unauthorised software prevented

guidance: Implement application allowlisting on high-risk endpoints and servers.
implementationGuide: Establish a formal change management process that ensures all changes to production systems are authorized, tested, and documented to prevent unauthorized modifications and reduce the risk of change-induced outages. Implement change management in ServiceNow ITSM, requiring all production changes to be submitted as change requests with a risk assessment, rollback plan, and CAB approval before implementation. Enforce automated change gates in your CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions or Jenkins) that block deployments lacking required approvals, linked test results, and security scan clearance. Monitor for unauthorized changes using AWS Config, Azure Policy, or CrowdStrike Falcon's file integrity monitoring, alerting the SOC within 30 minutes of detection. Conduct post-change reviews for all significant changes within 5 business days, documenting outcomes in ServiceNow ITSM. You are done when no production change is implemented without a documented, approved change request, unauthorized changes are detected within 30 minutes, and post-change reviews are completed on schedule. A common failure is emergency change processes that bypass security review, used routinely rather than exclusively in genuine emergencies.

title: Potentially adverse events analysed
description: Potentially adverse events are analysed to better characterise them and detect cybersecurity incidents.
guidance: Define and tune SIEM use cases to detect the top 10 attack techniques relevant to the organisation's threat profile.
implementationGuide: Establish processes for analysing detected events to determine whether they represent genuine security incidents, prioritizing investigation based on severity and potential impact. Implement a structured alert triage process in your SIEM (Splunk) or SOAR platform (Cortex XSOAR), with defined analyst workflows for each alert category that guide investigation steps and escalation criteria. Configure alert correlation rules in Splunk that combine low-confidence signals into higher-fidelity composite alerts, reducing analyst fatigue and focusing attention on true threats. Use threat intelligence enrichment from Recorded Future or MITRE ATT&CK to add context to alerts automatically before they reach the analyst. Define and enforce triage SLAs — critical alerts: 15 minutes, high: 1 hour — measured and reported monthly. You are done when every alert has a documented triage outcome within SLA, correlation rules reduce alert volume without reducing true positive detection, and threat intelligence enrichment is automated. A common failure is high alert volumes overwhelming analysts, causing critical alerts to be missed or triaged superficially without adequate investigation.

title: Information is correlated from multiple sources

guidance: Feed endpoint, network, identity, and cloud logs into the SIEM and correlate across sources in detection rules.
implementationGuide: Collect, correlate, and analyse security event data from across your environment to detect patterns and behaviours that indicate potential attacks or compromises. Deploy Splunk SIEM as your centralised event correlation platform, ingesting logs from endpoints, servers, network devices, cloud control planes, identity providers, and application logs. Build a detection rule library mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques, ensuring coverage across all major tactic categories (Initial Access through Impact), with coverage metrics reviewed quarterly. Implement UEBA in Splunk UEBA or Microsoft Sentinel to detect anomalous behaviour that rules alone would miss, such as unusual privileged account activity or lateral movement patterns. Conduct quarterly detection coverage reviews using MITRE ATT&CK Navigator to identify gaps and prioritize new rule development. You are done when your detection library covers all MITRE ATT&CK tactic categories, UEBA is operational for all critical user populations, and detection coverage is reviewed quarterly. A common failure is deploying a SIEM but relying on out-of-the-box rules without customizing them to your environment, producing high false-positive rates and missing environment-specific attack patterns.

title: Estimated impact and scope of adverse events understood
description: The estimated impact and scope of adverse events are understood.
guidance: Establish an initial impact assessment process to classify incident severity within the first 30 minutes of detection.
implementationGuide: Assess the potential impact of detected adverse events to prioritize response and allocate resources appropriately. Develop an impact assessment framework that considers asset criticality, data sensitivity, number of affected systems, and business process disruption, documented in your incident response policy. Integrate ServiceNow CMDB asset criticality data into your SIEM (Splunk) and SOAR (Cortex XSOAR) so that alerts automatically include the criticality of affected assets. Train SOC analysts to complete a structured impact assessment within 30 minutes of a confirmed incident, using a standardized form in Confluence that captures affected systems, data, and initial severity. Automate initial impact estimation in Cortex XSOAR using playbooks that query CMDB for affected asset context and populate the incident record. You are done when every confirmed incident has a documented impact assessment completed within 30 minutes, and asset criticality context is automatically included in all alerts. A common failure is treating all alerts at the same priority regardless of the criticality of the affected asset, wasting analyst time on low-impact events while high-impact incidents wait.

title: Information on adverse events provided to authorised staff
description: Information on adverse events is provided to authorised staff and tools.

Establish an incident notification matrix defining who must be notified at each severity level and within what timeframe, published in your incident response runbook in Confluence. Automate stakeholder notifications using PagerDuty for on-call rotations and Cortex XSOAR for structured incident communications, ensuring notification evidence is captured in the incident record. Participate in sector-specific information sharing forums (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC, CISA) to share relevant threat and incident information with peers, with sharing records maintained in Confluence. Define regulatory notification obligations and integrate them into your incident response runbook, with automated reminders in ServiceNow GRC when notification deadlines approach. You are done when every confirmed incident triggers appropriate stakeholder notifications within defined SLAs, regulatory notifications are tracked, and external threat sharing records are maintained. A common failure is delaying regulatory notifications because the legal obligation is not embedded in the incident response process, risking regulatory penalties.

title: Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information integrated
description: Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis.
guidance: Integrate threat intelligence IoCs into SIEM detection rules and enrich alerts with threat context automatically.
implementationGuide: Use cyber threat intelligence to proactively hunt for evidence of threats in your environment that have evaded automated detection, and integrate intelligence findings into your detection improvement cycle. Establish a threat hunting programme with dedicated analyst capacity, using a structured methodology aligned to MITRE ATT&CK, with hunts initiated based on prioritized threat intelligence. Use Splunk as the primary hunting platform, augmented by CrowdStrike Falcon's threat graph and endpoint telemetry for host-based hunting. Document each hunt in a standardized format in Confluence, capturing hypothesis, data sources queried, findings, and new detection rules created. Share hunt findings with the detection engineering team to drive new Splunk detection rule development, tracked in Jira. Conduct at minimum two threat hunts per quarter, measured against your hunting programme KPIs reported to the SOC manager. You are done when threat hunts are conducted at least quarterly, every hunt produces a documented outcome, and new detection rules are created from hunt findings. A common failure is running threat hunts as one-off reactive activities rather than a systematic programme with consistent methodology and documentation.
exampleEvidence: Threat hunting programme charter with methodology, cadence, and MITRE ATT&CK alignment documented
CrowdStrike Falcon threat graph and Splunk hunting query library with technique coverage map
Standardized hunt documentation in Confluence for each hunt conducted in the past 12 months
Jira tickets showing new Splunk detection rules created from threat hunt findings
Quarterly threat hunt completion report with KPI measurements presented to SOC management
Hunt-derived detection rule deployed to Splunk with false positive rate tracking since deployment

title: Networks monitored
description: Networks and network services are monitored to find potentially adverse events.
guidance: Deploy NDR or IDS on key network segments and review alerts daily.
implementationGuide: Continuously monitor your network infrastructure for anomalous activity, unauthorized connections, and signs of compromise, using both signature-based and behavioural detection approaches. Deploy an NDR tool such as Darktrace, Vectra AI, or Cisco Secure Network Analytics across all network segments, with traffic mirrored from core switches and cloud VPC flow logs ingested. Integrate NDR alerts into Splunk SIEM for correlation with endpoint and identity events, enabling multi-signal threat detection. Configure detection coverage for key threat patterns including C2 beaconing, lateral movement, data staging, and DNS tunnelling, validated against MITRE ATT&CK. Review NDR detection coverage and tune models quarterly to reduce false positives, with tuning actions documented in Jira. You are done when all production network segments are covered by continuous NDR monitoring, alerts integrate with Splunk, and detection models are reviewed and tuned quarterly. A common failure is monitoring only the perimeter while leaving east-west traffic between internal segments unmonitored, making lateral movement invisible.

NDR to Splunk integration configuration confirming alert forwarding and correlation rule configuration

VPC flow log ingestion configuration for cloud environments feeding NDR and Splunk

title: Physical environment monitored
description: The physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events.
guidance: Integrate physical access control logs and CCTV alerts into the security monitoring workflow.
implementationGuide: Monitor the physical environment of critical facilities — data centres, server rooms, and network closets — to detect unauthorized physical access, environmental threats, and tampering. Implement physical access control systems (PACS) from providers such as Lenel, Genetec, or HID for all critical facilities, with access logs forwarded to Splunk for correlation with logical access events. Deploy CCTV coverage at all entry points, server aisles, and equipment racks in critical facilities, with footage retained for a minimum of 90 days. Configure environmental monitoring sensors (temperature, humidity, power, water) using a platform such as Vertiv or Schneider EcoStruxure, with alerts routing to facilities and security operations teams via PagerDuty. Conduct quarterly physical access audits reviewing PACS logs for unauthorized or anomalous access attempts, with findings tracked in Jira. You are done when all critical facilities have PACS, CCTV, and environmental monitoring with logs integrated into Splunk and quarterly audits are completed. A common failure is maintaining robust logical security controls while neglecting physical monitoring, allowing physical access to bypass all logical controls.

title: Personnel activity monitored
description: Personnel activity and technology usage are monitored to find potentially adverse events.
guidance: Deploy UEBA capabilities to detect insider threat patterns; ensure monitoring is communicated to staff per policy.
implementationGuide: Monitor personnel activity on critical systems to detect insider threats, compromised accounts, and policy violations, balancing security monitoring with privacy requirements. Deploy UEBA using Splunk UEBA, Microsoft Sentinel, or Exabeam to baseline normal user behaviour and alert on significant deviations such as mass data download, off-hours access to sensitive systems, or unusual privilege escalation. Enable privileged user monitoring through CyberArk session recording for all administrator accounts accessing critical systems, with recordings retained for 12 months. Define a personnel monitoring policy reviewed by HR and legal, published in ServiceNow GRC, and communicated to all employees to meet transparency and privacy obligations. Configure Splunk to alert on defined insider threat indicators — such as access following a resignation notice or after-hours access to sensitive data — with SOC analyst review within 1 hour. You are done when UEBA is operational for all high-risk user populations, privileged sessions are recorded, and the monitoring policy is documented, reviewed by legal, and communicated to employees. A common failure is deploying personnel monitoring without a written policy reviewed by legal, creating legal exposure for the organization.
exampleEvidence: Splunk UEBA, Microsoft Sentinel, or Exabeam behavioural baseline configuration for high-risk user populations

title: External service provider activities monitored
description: External service provider activities and services are monitored to find potentially adverse events.
guidance: Review third-party access logs monthly and include supplier monitoring requirements in contracts.
implementationGuide: Monitor the activities of external service providers and third-party connections to detect unauthorized or anomalous access that could indicate a supply chain compromise or vendor abuse. Enforce separate network zones for all third-party connections, monitored independently using your NDR tool (Darktrace or Vectra AI) and Splunk, with alerts for anomalous behaviour in those zones. Require all third-party remote access to use an organization-managed privileged access workstation (PAW) or vendor-specific remote access solution such as BeyondTrust or CyberArk Alero, with full session recording. Review vendor access logs monthly, comparing access volumes and times against contracted activity schedules, with anomalies investigated and tracked in Jira. Integrate third-party access events into your SIEM to correlate with other security events and enable detection of supply chain attack patterns. You are done when all third-party access is through a monitored, recorded channel, access logs are reviewed monthly, and anomalous activity triggers automated alerts. A common failure is granting third-party vendors broad VPN access that blends their traffic with internal traffic, making their activity unmonitorable.
exampleEvidence: Network zone diagram showing dedicated third-party access segments with NDR monitoring and Splunk ingestion

title: Computing hardware and software monitored
description: Computing hardware and software, runtime environments, and their data are monitored to find potentially adverse events.
guidance: Deploy EDR on all endpoints and enable real-time telemetry ingestion into the SIEM.
implementationGuide: Monitor your computing infrastructure — including cloud workloads, containers, and serverless functions — for misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, and signs of compromise. Deploy a CSPM tool such as Wiz, Prisma Cloud, or Microsoft Defender for Cloud to continuously assess all cloud accounts for misconfigurations, compliance violations, and active threats, with findings forwarded to Splunk. Implement runtime security for containers using Falco, Aqua Security, or Sysdig Secure to detect anomalous container behaviour, escaped processes, and unauthorized network connections. Monitor infrastructure change events using AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, and GCP Cloud Audit Logs, ingested into Splunk with detection rules for unauthorized or anomalous changes. Conduct monthly cloud security posture reviews using CSPM dashboards, tracking open findings in Jira with SLA-based remediation. You are done when all cloud workloads, containers, and serverless functions are covered by continuous monitoring, CSPM findings are tracked to closure, and runtime security detects anomalous container behaviour. A common failure is monitoring infrastructure at the perimeter while leaving cloud-native workloads such as serverless functions and managed services without runtime monitoring.
exampleEvidence: Wiz, Prisma Cloud, or Microsoft Defender for Cloud CSPM coverage report showing all cloud accounts monitored

Monthly cloud security posture review report with CSPM finding trends and Jira remediation tracker

title: Incident response plan executed
description: The incident response plan is executed in coordination with relevant third parties once an incident is declared.
guidance: Maintain an IRP with named roles, escalation contacts, and playbooks for the top 5 incident types.
implementationGuide: Establish and maintain a formal incident response plan that defines the process for detecting, responding to, and recovering from cybersecurity incidents across all severity levels. Develop the IR plan following NIST SP 800-61 or SANS PICERL framework, stored in Confluence as the authoritative reference, with version-controlled updates requiring CISO approval. Publish and communicate the IR plan to all relevant stakeholders — SOC, IT operations, legal, communications, and executive leadership — with acknowledgement records in Vanta. Integrate the IR plan with PagerDuty for automated on-call notification and Cortex XSOAR for playbook-driven response execution. Review and update the IR plan at least annually and after each major incident, with updates tracked in Jira. You are done when the IR plan is current, published, acknowledged by all stakeholders, and integrated with your automation platforms. A common failure is maintaining an IR plan in a shared drive that is never rehearsed, making it a theoretical document rather than an operational guide.
exampleEvidence: Incident response plan document following NIST SP 800-61 or SANS PICERL with CISO approval and version history

title: Incidents triaged
description: Incidents are triaged based on established criteria.
guidance: Define a severity matrix (P1–P4) and train the service desk and SOC to classify incidents within 15 minutes of detection.
implementationGuide: Ensure that incidents are triaged, classified, and assigned to the appropriate response team in a timely manner so that resources are focused on the highest-impact events. Implement a structured incident classification scheme in your SIEM (Splunk) and SOAR (Cortex XSOAR) that assigns a severity level to each incident based on impact, asset criticality, and threat type. Configure Cortex XSOAR to automatically classify and assign incidents based on defined criteria, routing to the correct SOC tier or specialist team within 15 minutes of detection. Define escalation procedures in Jira Service Management or ServiceNow ITSM, with automated escalation when response SLAs are breached. Maintain and review incident classification accuracy metrics monthly, adjusting classification rules where false escalations or under-escalations are identified. You are done when all incidents are classified within 15 minutes, SLA compliance is measured and reported monthly, and escalation procedures are tested quarterly. A common failure is classifying all incidents as the same severity to avoid escalation complexity, preventing critical incidents from receiving the resources they require.
exampleEvidence: Incident classification scheme documentation defining severity levels with impact and asset criticality criteria

title: Incidents categorised
description: Incidents are categorised and reported consistent with the response plan.
guidance: Map incident categories (malware, data breach, DDoS, insider) to corresponding playbooks.
implementationGuide: Ensure that incident response activities are coordinated across all relevant internal teams and external parties so that response is coherent, efficient, and well-documented. Establish an Incident Response Team (IRT) with defined roles — Incident Commander, Technical Lead, Communications Lead, Legal Counsel, Executive Sponsor — documented in your IR plan in Confluence. Use a war room communication platform such as Slack or Microsoft Teams with a dedicated incident channel per active incident, integrated with Cortex XSOAR to auto-create channels on incident declaration. Define coordination procedures with external parties including your cyber insurer, external forensics retainer (Mandiant or CrowdStrike Services), law enforcement liaison, and regulators, stored in your IR plan. Conduct bi-annual tabletop exercises that include all IRT roles and selected external parties to validate coordination procedures, with after-action reports tracked in Jira. You are done when IRT roles are defined and rehearsed, war room coordination is tested, and external coordination procedures are documented and current. A common failure is exercising IR with the technical team only, discovering during a real incident that legal, communications, and executive coordination breaks down.
exampleEvidence: Incident Response Team charter with defined roles, responsibilities, and contact information in Confluence

title: Incidents escalated or elevated
description: Incidents are escalated or elevated as needed.
guidance: Define escalation criteria (e.g. P1 = immediate CISO notification, regulatory reporting within 72 hours) and test annually.
implementationGuide: Ensure that incidents are resolved effectively and that recovery actions restore normal operations securely and completely, without reintroducing the initial vulnerability. Define resolution criteria for each incident severity level in your IR plan, requiring sign-off from the Incident Commander, Technical Lead, and relevant system owner before declaring an incident closed. Implement a structured eradication and recovery checklist in Cortex XSOAR covering threat removal, vulnerability remediation, system restoration from known-good backups, and post-recovery validation scanning. Validate recovery completeness using Tenable.io to scan restored systems for vulnerabilities before reconnecting them to the production network. Track incident resolution metrics — mean time to resolve (MTTR) by severity — in Splunk dashboards reported monthly to security leadership. You are done when every incident has documented eradication and recovery steps, MTTR is measured by severity, and restored systems are validated before production reconnection. A common failure is declaring incidents closed after removing visible indicators of compromise without validating that the root cause has been addressed, allowing re-compromise through the same vector.

title: Internal and external stakeholders notified
description: Internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents.
guidance: Define a communications plan for incidents specifying who is notified, when, and what template is used for each severity level.
implementationGuide: Establish formal internal incident communication procedures ensuring that relevant stakeholders are informed during and after incidents in a consistent, timely manner appropriate to their role. Define internal communication templates for each severity level in Confluence — technical updates for the SOC, executive summaries for leadership, and business impact statements for affected business units. Configure PagerDuty to automatically send initial incident notifications to defined distribution groups within 15 minutes of incident declaration, with automated follow-up updates every 30 minutes for active P1/P2 incidents. Integrate Cortex XSOAR with your collaboration platform (Slack or Teams) to post automated status updates to designated incident channels as response milestones are reached. Conduct bi-annual communication drills as part of tabletop exercises, testing message clarity and timing, with findings tracked in Jira. You are done when communication templates are defined for all severity levels, automated notifications are active, and communication timing meets defined SLAs in every exercise. A common failure is communicating technical details to executives who need business impact context, and business impact summaries to engineers who need technical detail, causing confusion and misaligned decisions.
exampleEvidence: Internal incident communication template library in Confluence for each severity level and audience type

title: Information shared with designated parties
description: Information is shared with designated internal and external stakeholders as authorised.
guidance: Establish information sharing agreements with sector peers, regulators, and ISACs and use them during significant incidents.
implementationGuide: Share information about cybersecurity incidents with external parties — regulators, customers, partners, and law enforcement — in accordance with legal obligations, contractual requirements, and voluntary sharing commitments. Develop an external notification policy published in ServiceNow GRC that maps each incident type to the applicable regulatory notification obligation (GDPR 72-hour, SEC 4-day, state breach laws) and includes approved notification templates. Maintain retainer agreements with external legal counsel specializing in incident response and breach notification, contactable 24/7, with contact details in your IR plan. Participate in CISA's Automated Indicator Sharing (AIS) or sector-specific ISAC to share technical indicators of compromise following incidents, with sharing records maintained. Conduct annual breach notification drills with legal, communications, and executive teams, testing notification content, timing, and approval workflows, with findings in Jira. You are done when every regulatory notification obligation is documented, templates are prepared and legally reviewed, and drills confirm the process can meet all timing requirements. A common failure is initiating regulatory notifications without legal review, resulting in incomplete or premature disclosures that create additional liability.

title: Analysis performed to establish root cause
description: Analysis is performed to establish what has taken place during an incident and the root cause of the incident.
guidance: Conduct a root cause analysis (RCA) for all P1/P2 incidents and publish findings within 10 working days.
implementationGuide: Conduct thorough analysis of cybersecurity incidents to establish root cause, scope, and timeline, enabling effective remediation and prevention of recurrence. Implement a structured root cause analysis (RCA) methodology — such as the 5 Whys or fishbone diagram — required for all incidents above a defined severity threshold, with a standard template in Confluence. Conduct forensic analysis using CrowdStrike Falcon's endpoint telemetry, Splunk log search, and memory analysis tools to reconstruct the attack timeline and identify patient-zero, initial access vector, and lateral movement paths. Engage external forensic firms (Mandiant or CrowdStrike Services) for major incidents where internal capacity is insufficient, with a retainer agreement in place. Document RCA findings and the full attack timeline in the incident record in ServiceNow ITSM, and share sanitized summaries with the broader security team for organizational learning. You are done when every qualifying incident has a completed RCA with a documented root cause, attack timeline, and improvement recommendations, delivered within 14 days of incident closure. A common failure is stopping analysis at the proximate cause — the exploited vulnerability — without investigating why the vulnerability existed and why detection was delayed.

title: Incidents contained
description: Incidents are contained.
guidance: Define containment actions per incident category in playbooks; practice them in quarterly tabletop exercises.
implementationGuide: Contain the impact of active incidents by isolating affected systems, blocking malicious activity, and preventing the threat from spreading to additional systems or data. Develop and maintain containment playbooks for each major incident type (ransomware, BEC, data exfiltration, insider threat) in Cortex XSOAR, with automated and manual steps clearly delineated. Enable CrowdStrike Falcon's network containment to isolate compromised endpoints with a single click from the Falcon console, executable by analysts without requiring manager approval for confirmed P1/P2 incidents. Configure Palo Alto Networks firewall to support emergency rule deployment — blocking specific IP ranges or domains — within 15 minutes of analyst request, with a documented emergency rule process. Test containment playbooks quarterly through purple team exercises, measuring time-to-contain from initial detection, and report results to SOC management. You are done when containment playbooks exist for all major incident types, CrowdStrike containment is executable within 5 minutes, and quarterly tests confirm target time-to-contain SLAs are met. A common failure is requiring multi-level approval for containment actions during an active incident, adding hours of delay while the attacker continues lateral movement.

Containment execution log from a real or simulated incident showing isolation timestamps

title: Incidents eradicated
description: Incidents are eradicated.
guidance: Document eradication steps in each playbook and confirm eradication before moving to recovery phase.
implementationGuide: Eradicate threats from your environment following containment by removing malicious code, closing exploited vulnerabilities, eliminating unauthorized access, and validating that the threat is fully removed. Document eradication procedures for each incident type in Cortex XSOAR playbooks, including malware removal, credential resets, patch deployment, and account disablement steps. Use CrowdStrike Falcon to perform threat hunting across all endpoints after initial containment, identifying additional compromised systems before eradication begins. Require Tenable.io vulnerability scans of all systems involved in an incident before they are returned to production, confirming that exploited vulnerabilities are patched. Mandate a full credential reset for all accounts that were compromised or had access to affected systems, executed through Okta or Microsoft Entra ID, with completion verified before restoration. You are done when every eradication action is documented in the incident record, Tenable.io confirms vulnerability remediation, and credential resets are completed before restoration. A common failure is eradicating the initial access point while missing additional footholds established during lateral movement, allowing the attacker to persist through the incident response.
exampleEvidence: Cortex XSOAR eradication playbook for each major incident type with required completion checkpoints
CrowdStrike Falcon threat hunt report confirming scope of compromise across all endpoints before eradication

Okta or Microsoft Entra ID credential reset completion record for all compromised or exposed accounts
Incident eradication completion sign-off record with Technical Lead and Incident Commander approval

title: Recovery plan executed
description: The recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed once initiated from the incident response process.
guidance: Maintain documented recovery runbooks for each critical system; test them at least annually.
implementationGuide: Develop and maintain a comprehensive recovery plan that defines the procedures, priorities, and resources required to restore normal operations following a cybersecurity incident. Develop the recovery plan as part of your business continuity and disaster recovery (BC/DR) programme, maintained in Confluence and reviewed by the CISO, CIO, and business continuity manager annually. Define recovery priorities based on asset criticality tiers from ServiceNow CMDB, ensuring critical business services are restored first, with documented RTOs and RPOs. Test recovery procedures at least annually through a full DR exercise that includes actual system restoration from backup, measuring time against RTO targets and documenting results in Jira. Integrate recovery plan procedures into Cortex XSOAR playbooks for automated orchestration of initial recovery steps such as backup restoration initiation and system health checks. You are done when the recovery plan is current, tested annually with results meeting RTO targets, and integrated with automation platforms. A common failure is maintaining a recovery plan that has never been tested, discovering during an actual incident that RTOs are unachievable with the documented procedures.
exampleEvidence: Recovery plan document with CISO, CIO, and BCM annual review sign-offs and version history in Confluence
ServiceNow CMDB criticality tiers mapped to recovery priorities with RTOs and RPOs documented
Annual DR exercise report showing actual restoration times compared to RTO targets for critical systems
Cortex XSOAR recovery orchestration playbook automating initial recovery steps with execution log
Jira DR exercise finding tickets with remediation actions and closure evidence
Recovery plan distribution record confirming all relevant teams have acknowledged current version

title: Recovery actions selected
description: Recovery actions are selected, scoped, prioritised, and performed.
guidance: Prioritise recovery order based on criticality tiers from the BIA; document the recovery sequence.
implementationGuide: Execute recovery activities in a controlled, prioritized sequence that restores critical business functions first and validates system integrity before reconnecting recovered systems to the production environment. Define a recovery execution sequence in your recovery plan based on business service criticality, with each step documented as a checklist in Cortex XSOAR playbooks. Restore systems from verified clean backups using Veeam or AWS Backup, confirming backup integrity hashes before restoration begins. Perform integrity validation on all restored systems using CrowdStrike Falcon and Tenable.io before production reconnection, confirming no malware persistence and no unpatched vulnerabilities. Implement a recovery staging environment where restored systems are validated before they rejoin the production network, documented in your recovery runbook. You are done when every recovery execution follows the documented sequence, integrity validation is completed before reconnection, and recovery times are measured against RTO targets. A common failure is restoring infected backups because backup integrity was not validated before restoration, reintroducing the threat into the recovered environment.
exampleEvidence: Recovery execution sequence checklist in Cortex XSOAR aligned to business service criticality tiers

title: Integrity of recovered assets verified
description: The integrity of recovered assets is verified.
guidance: Validate recovered systems using known-good baselines and checksums before returning to production.
implementationGuide: Verify that recovered systems and services are functioning correctly and securely after an incident, and confirm that normal operations have been restored before declaring recovery complete. Define formal recovery verification criteria for each critical service in your recovery plan, including functional tests, security scans, and user acceptance validation. Execute post-recovery functional tests using automated test suites where available (Selenium, Postman, or application-specific smoke tests) and document results in Confluence. Conduct a Tenable.io vulnerability scan of all recovered systems, confirming that no critical or high vulnerabilities remain before reconnection. Require system owner sign-off confirming that the system is performing as expected before the CISO declares recovery complete, recorded in ServiceNow ITSM. Communicate recovery completion to all stakeholders through the defined incident communication channels, closing the incident record. You are done when all recovery verification criteria are met, system owners have signed off, and incident closure is formally documented with stakeholder notification. A common failure is declaring recovery complete based on infrastructure metrics alone without validating that business functions are operating correctly from the user's perspective.
exampleEvidence: Recovery verification criteria for each critical service defined in the recovery plan

title: Recovery activities communicated
description: Recovery activities and progress in restoring operational capabilities are communicated to designated internal and external stakeholders.
guidance: Provide regular recovery status updates to senior leadership and affected stakeholders until full restoration.
implementationGuide: Manage external communications during and after recovery to maintain trust, meet legal obligations, and support organizational reputation with customers, regulators, and the public. Develop a crisis communications plan as part of your IR and recovery programme, maintained in Confluence and reviewed by legal, communications, and executive leadership annually. Prepare holding statements and template communications for common incident scenarios, reviewed by legal counsel and stored in ServiceNow GRC with approval dates. Assign a designated spokesperson for all external media and regulatory communications, with backup contacts, documented in the crisis communications plan. Coordinate all external communications through your communications team and legal counsel, with a documented approval workflow before any external statement is released. Conduct annual crisis communications drills including media simulation, testing message clarity, approval workflow speed, and regulatory notification timing. You are done when the crisis communications plan is current, templates are legally approved, external communications go through the defined approval workflow, and annual drills confirm process effectiveness. A common failure is allowing technical staff to communicate directly with media or regulators without legal and communications oversight, creating liability and reputational risk.

title: Public updates managed
description: Public updates on the incident are shared using approved methods and messaging.
guidance: Assign a communications lead for external messaging and pre-approve response templates for common incident types.
implementationGuide: Restore organizational reputation and stakeholder trust following a cybersecurity incident by demonstrating effective response, transparent communication, and improved security posture. Develop a reputation recovery plan as part of your BC/DR programme that includes customer communications, media engagement, regulatory updates, and evidence of remediation, reviewed by the CISO and communications director. Provide affected customers and partners with clear, honest post-incident communications detailing what happened, what was done, and what improvements have been made, developed with legal review. Commission an independent post-incident security assessment to validate that remediation is complete, sharing the executive summary with key stakeholders as evidence of restored posture. Achieve or renew relevant security certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001) following significant incidents to provide third-party validation of restored controls, documented in Vanta. Monitor brand sentiment and stakeholder feedback following public disclosure, with findings reported to executive leadership monthly during the recovery period. You are done when the reputation recovery plan is executed, customer communications are sent with legal sign-off, an independent assessment is complete, and stakeholder feedback shows recovering trust. A common failure is focusing entirely on technical recovery while neglecting stakeholder trust recovery, resulting in customer churn and regulatory scrutiny that exceeds the cost of the original incident.
exampleEvidence: Reputation recovery plan reviewed by CISO and communications director with defined activities and timelines
